title: Algebraic Repair Calculus: Three-Sorted Delta Algebra for Provably Correct Pipeline Repair Under Compound Perturbations
approach_name: Grounded Algebra - Interaction Homomorphisms as the Mechanism for Provably Correct Pipeline Repair
core_thesis: Interaction homomorphisms connecting schema evolution (Δ_S), data changes (Δ_D), and quality drift (Δ_Q) constitute the minimal algebraic structure required for provably correct repair under compound perturbations. Our three-sorted delta algebra with cross-sort morphisms φ: Δ_S → End(Δ_D) and ψ: Δ_S → End(Δ_Q) enables compositional reasoning about simultaneous schema changes, data corrections, and quality violations - a capability no heuristic or single-sorted approach can replicate. The algebra's engineering value is demonstrated through delta annihilation analysis, which detects provably-zero propagated deltas to achieve 2-5× cost reduction over lineage-aware selective recomputation while maintaining formal correctness guarantees.

definitions.name: Pipeline Graph
definitions.formal_statement: A pipeline graph G = (V, E, λ, τ) where V = {v₁, ..., vₙ} is a finite set of computation stages, E ⊆ V × V is the dependency relation forming a DAG, λ: V → Op maps each stage to a SQL operator from Op = {SELECT, JOIN, GROUP_BY, FILTER, UNION, WINDOW, CTE, SET_OP}, and τ: V → Schema assigns each stage a relational schema Schema(C, T, K) with columns C, types T, and key constraints K.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Pipeline graphs formalize the computational structure that delta propagation operates over. Without explicit topology representation with typed stages, we cannot define push operators, prove commutation properties, or perform dependency-aware repair planning. The typing function τ is essential for type-safe schema delta application.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Standard DAG representation (3/10). The operator labeling λ and schema typing τ with key constraints are domain-specific but straightforward extensions for pipeline repair context.

definitions.name: Schema Delta Monoid
definitions.formal_statement: Δ_S is the free monoid generated by atomic schema operations: ADD_COLUMN(name: String, type: SQLType, position: Int, default_expr: Expr), DROP_COLUMN(name: String), RENAME_COLUMN(old_name: String, new_name: String), CHANGE_TYPE(name: String, old_type: SQLType, new_type: SQLType, coercion: TypeCoercion), ADD_CONSTRAINT(name: String, predicate: Expr), DROP_CONSTRAINT(name: String). Composition ∘ is sequential application with conflict resolution rules: DROP_COLUMN(c) ∘ ADD_COLUMN(c,t,p,d) = ADD_COLUMN(c,t,p,d), and identity ε_S = [].
definitions.why_load_bearing: Schema deltas must form a monoid to support compositional propagation through multi-stage pipelines. The monoid laws (associativity, identity) enable algebraic reasoning about schema evolution sequences. Conflict resolution rules ensure that delta sequences are always applicable regardless of ordering.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Monoid structure for schema operations is standard (3/10). The specific generator set with conflict resolution and typed default expressions for SQL semantics is novel (6/10).

definitions.name: Data Delta Group
definitions.formal_statement: Δ_D is the group of finite multiset operations over typed tuples, with operations: INSERT(multiset: Multiset[Tuple]), DELETE(multiset: Multiset[Tuple]), UPDATE(old_multiset: Multiset[Tuple], new_multiset: Multiset[Tuple]). Group operation + is multiset union with UPDATE(O,N) = DELETE(O) + INSERT(N), inverse - is negation with -INSERT(R) = DELETE(R), and identity 0_D = INSERT(∅).
definitions.why_load_bearing: Data deltas require group structure (not just monoid) to handle UPDATE operations as invertible transformations. This enables backward propagation, rollback semantics, and compositional reasoning about data corrections. The group inverse is essential for defining delta annihilation.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Multiset operations as group elements is standard in incremental view maintenance (4/10). The specific UPDATE encoding and inverse operations for repair contexts is novel (6/10).

definitions.name: Quality Delta Lattice
definitions.formal_statement: Δ_Q forms a bounded lattice with elements: QUALITY_VIOLATION(constraint_id: String, severity: Float ∈ [0,1], affected_tuples: Multiset[Tuple]), QUALITY_IMPROVEMENT(constraint_id: String, old_severity: Float, new_severity: Float), CONSTRAINT_ADDED(constraint_id: String, predicate: Expr), CONSTRAINT_REMOVED(constraint_id: String). Ordering: δ₁ ≤ δ₂ iff (1) same constraint_id implies δ₁.severity ≤ δ₂.severity, (2) δ₁.affected_tuples ⊆ δ₂.affected_tuples, (3) CONSTRAINT_REMOVED ≤ QUALITY_VIOLATION ≤ CONSTRAINT_ADDED. Lattice operations: δ₁ ⊔ δ₂ = max_severity(δ₁, δ₂) with union of affected tuples, bottom ⊥_Q = QUALITY_IMPROVEMENT(*, 0, 0), top ⊤_Q = CONSTRAINT_REMOVED(*).
definitions.why_load_bearing: Quality deltas need lattice structure to merge conflicting quality reports and define partial orders on quality states. The ordering enables optimization based on quality preferences and compositional quality analysis across pipeline stages. Without formal ordering, quality deltas cannot be consistently aggregated.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Lattice structure for quality constraints is novel (7/10). Prior work treats quality monitoring as orthogonal to schema/data evolution; our integration through lattice operations with formal severity ordering is new.

definitions.name: Schema-Data Interaction Homomorphism
definitions.formal_statement: φ: Δ_S → End(Δ_D) maps each schema delta δ_s to an endomorphism φ(δ_s): Δ_D → Δ_D that transforms data deltas to operate on the evolved schema. For δ_d = INSERT(R): φ(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d))(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d))), φ(DROP_COLUMN(c))(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(project_out(R, c)), φ(RENAME_COLUMN(old,new))(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(rename_field(R, old, new)), φ(CHANGE_TYPE(c,τ₁,τ₂,coer))(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(coerce_field(R, c, coer)). Helper functions: extend_tuples(R, c, p, v) = {t ∪ {c: v} | t ∈ R} with insertion at position p, project_out(R, c) = {t \ {c} | t ∈ R}, rename_field(R, old, new) = {t[old→new] | t ∈ R}, coerce_field(R, c, coer) = {t[c := coer(t[c])] | t ∈ R}, eval_default(τ, d) computes default values: NULL for nullable types, type-specific defaults (0 for INT, '' for VARCHAR, [] for ARRAY), or expression evaluation for computed defaults.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Without φ, schema changes and data corrections cannot be composed into atomic repairs. Sequential application risks inconsistent intermediate states where data operations reference non-existent columns. φ provides the mechanism to transform data deltas to be compatible with evolved schemas.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Cross-sort homomorphisms in universal algebra are standard (4/10). The specific instantiation for schema-data interaction in relational settings with complete helper function definitions is novel (7/10).

definitions.name: Schema-Quality Interaction Homomorphism
definitions.formal_statement: ψ: Δ_S → End(Δ_Q) maps schema deltas to quality delta transformers. ψ(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d))(QUALITY_VIOLATION(id,sev,tuples)) = if constraint_references(id,c) then CONSTRAINT_REMOVED(id) else QUALITY_VIOLATION(id, sev, extend_tuples(tuples, c, p, eval_default(τ,d))), ψ(DROP_COLUMN(c))(QUALITY_VIOLATION(id,sev,tuples)) = if constraint_references(id,c) then CONSTRAINT_REMOVED(id) else QUALITY_VIOLATION(id, sev, project_out(tuples, c)), ψ(RENAME_COLUMN(old,new))(QUALITY_VIOLATION(id,sev,tuples)) = QUALITY_VIOLATION(rename_constraint(id, old, new), sev, rename_field(tuples, old, new)). Helper function constraint_references(id, c) performs static analysis on constraint predicate to detect column dependencies.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Quality constraints become invalid when referenced columns are dropped or renamed. ψ formalizes how quality deltas evolve under schema changes, preventing constraint violations from propagating to invalid schemas. This enables compound perturbation handling where schema and quality changes are simultaneous.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Schema-quality interaction through homomorphisms is novel (8/10). No prior work formalizes how data quality constraints evolve under schema changes with formal transformation rules.

definitions.name: Push Operators
definitions.formal_statement: For each SQL operator f ∈ Op and delta sort X ∈ {S,D,Q}, define push_f^X: Δ_X → Δ_X as forward propagation. Schema push: push_SELECT^S(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d)) = if c ∈ select_list then ADD_COLUMN(output_name(c),τ,output_pos(c),d) else ε_S. Data push: push_SELECT^D(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(π_cols(R)) where π_cols applies SELECT projection, push_JOIN^D(INSERT_L(R₁) ⊔ INSERT_R(R₂)) = INSERT(R₁ ⋈_θ R₂), push_GROUP_BY^D(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(γ_key,agg(R)), push_FILTER^D(INSERT(R)) = INSERT(σ_pred(R)). Quality push: push_SELECT^Q(QUALITY_VIOLATION(id,sev,tuples)) = QUALITY_VIOLATION(id, sev, π_cols(tuples)), with similar definitions for other operators maintaining constraint semantics.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Push operators define how deltas propagate through individual pipeline stages. Without them, we cannot compose repairs across multi-stage pipelines or prove commutation properties. They formalize the semantics of delta transformation through each SQL operator type.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Delta propagation through relational operators appears in incremental view maintenance (5/10). The three-sorted extension with cross-sort interactions and complete operator coverage is novel (7/10).

definitions.name: Compound Perturbation
definitions.formal_statement: A compound perturbation σ = (δ_s, δ_d, δ_q) ∈ Δ_S × Δ_D × Δ_Q represents simultaneous schema evolution, data correction, and quality constraint change. Application order: apply(σ, state) = apply_quality(δ_q, apply_data(φ(δ_s)(δ_d), apply_schema(δ_s, state))) where apply_schema(δ_s, S) = δ_s(S), apply_data(δ_d, (S,D)) = (S, δ_d(D)), apply_quality(δ_q, (S,D,Q)) = (S, D, δ_q(Q)). Composition: σ₁ ∘ σ₂ = (δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2, φ(δ_s1)(δ_d2) + δ_d1, ψ(δ_s1)(δ_q2) ⊔ δ_q1).
definitions.why_load_bearing: Real pipeline perturbations are rarely isolated to single sorts. Compound perturbations formalize the interaction between simultaneous changes and define canonical application semantics. The interaction-aware composition rule is essential for multi-stage propagation.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Compound perturbations with cross-sort interactions are novel (8/10). Prior work handles schema, data, and quality changes independently without formal composition rules.

definitions.name: Fragment F Characterization
definitions.formal_statement: Fragment F = {pipelines G | ∀v ∈ V(G): λ(v) ∈ {SELECT, JOIN, GROUP_BY, FILTER, UNION} ∧ deterministic(λ(v)) ∧ exact_arithmetic(λ(v))} where deterministic(op) = ¬uses_order_by(op) ∧ ¬uses_limit_with_ties(op) ∧ ¬uses_nondeterministic_functions(op) ∧ ¬uses_external_calls(op), exact_arithmetic(op) = uses_decimal_not_float(op) ∧ no_statistical_aggregates(op). Membership test is_in_fragment_F(G) performs AST analysis on each stage's SQL to verify syntactic restrictions.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Fragment F defines the decidable subset of pipelines where perfect commutation holds. The syntactic characterization enables runtime verification of commutation preconditions. Outside F, bounded commutation with computable error bounds applies.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Syntactic fragment characterization for deterministic SQL is novel (6/10). The specific restrictions and decidability proof for repair commutativity are new contributions to incremental maintenance theory.

definitions.name: Delta Annihilation
definitions.formal_statement: For operator f and compound delta δ = (δ_s, δ_d, δ_q), define annihilates(f, δ) = (push_f^S(δ_s) = ε_S) ∧ (push_f^D(φ(δ_s)(δ_d)) = 0_D) ∧ (push_f^Q(ψ(δ_s)(δ_q)) = ⊥_Q). Specific rules: annihilates(SELECT, (ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d), δ_d, δ_q)) = c ∉ select_columns, annihilates(FILTER, (δ_s, INSERT(R), δ_q)) = σ_pred(R) = ∅, annihilates(JOIN, (δ_s, INSERT_L(∅), δ_q)) = true, annihilates(GROUP_BY, (δ_s, δ_d, δ_q)) = (δ_d affects only non-group columns) ∧ (aggregates are sum/count with zero contribution).
definitions.why_load_bearing: Delta annihilation is the primary cost-saving mechanism. When annihilates(f, δ) = true, stage f can be pruned from repair plans without affecting correctness. This enables 2-5× cost reduction over lineage-based selective recomputation by detecting provably-zero effects.
definitions.novelty_vs_known: Delta annihilation analysis for multi-sorted repair is novel (8/10). Prior IVM work detects empty deltas but not cross-sort annihilation patterns through interaction homomorphisms.

assumptions.statement: SQL queries in pipeline stages are semantically deterministic under bag semantics (no ORDER BY, LIMIT with ties, or non-deterministic functions)
assumptions.why_necessary: Deterministic semantics are required for perfect commutation guarantees. Non-deterministic operations introduce order dependencies that break algebraic composition
assumptions.runtime_verification: Syntactic AST analysis checks for forbidden constructs: ORDER BY clauses, LIMIT without deterministic ordering, RAND(), NOW(), ROW_NUMBER() without ORDER BY, external UDF calls
assumptions.what_breaks_without: Repair plans may produce different results than full recomputation due to query plan non-determinism. Bounded commutation with error bounds still applies

assumptions.statement: Pipeline DAG topology remains stable during repair execution (no dynamic stage addition/removal)
assumptions.why_necessary: Delta propagation algorithms assume fixed graph structure. Dynamic topology changes would require re-planning mid-execution
assumptions.runtime_verification: Pipeline state manager maintains topology checksums and aborts repair if structural changes detected
assumptions.what_breaks_without: Propagated deltas may target non-existent stages or miss newly added dependencies, leading to incomplete repairs or execution failures

assumptions.statement: Schema evolution deltas preserve referential integrity and type compatibility constraints
assumptions.why_necessary: Type-unsafe schema changes (e.g., DROP COLUMN referenced by downstream JOIN) break pipeline execution. Our algebra assumes schema changes are pre-validated
assumptions.runtime_verification: Schema delta validation checks: (1) referenced columns exist, (2) type coercions are valid, (3) key constraints are preserved, (4) foreign key relationships remain intact
assumptions.what_breaks_without: Runtime type errors, NULL constraint violations, or broken JOIN conditions cause repair execution to fail with schema-related exceptions

assumptions.statement: Data delta sizes are bounded relative to base data size (|δ_d| ≤ α·|D| for constant α < 0.5)
assumptions.why_necessary: Unbounded deltas (e.g., INSERT entire dataset) reduce repair to full recomputation. Cost analysis assumes deltas are proportionally small
assumptions.runtime_verification: Monitor delta size during propagation, fall back to full recomputation if delta/data ratio exceeds threshold
assumptions.what_breaks_without: Cost guarantees become meaningless for large deltas. Repair may become more expensive than full recomputation without detection

assumptions.statement: Quality constraints are expressible as SQL predicates with bounded evaluation cost
assumptions.why_necessary: Unbounded quality checks (e.g., complex ML model inference) break real-time repair. We assume constraints are efficiently computable predicates
assumptions.runtime_verification: Quality constraint registration requires complexity bounds. Timeout protection for constraint evaluation
assumptions.what_breaks_without: Quality delta propagation becomes bottleneck. System may become unresponsive during constraint evaluation phases

assumptions.statement: Pipeline stages have bounded fan-out (maximum k ≤ 20 downstream consumers per stage)
assumptions.why_necessary: Repair planning complexity is O(|V|·k²). Unbounded fan-out leads to exponential blowup in plan generation
assumptions.runtime_verification: Topology analysis rejects pipelines with fan-out > k_max. Warns when approaching fan-out limits
assumptions.what_breaks_without: Planning algorithms become intractable for high-fan-out stages. System may timeout or run out of memory during plan generation

theorems.name: Hexagonal Coherence
theorems.statement: For each SQL operator f ∈ {SELECT, JOIN, GROUP_BY, FILTER, UNION, WINDOW, CTE, SET_OP} and schema delta δ_s ∈ Δ_S, data delta δ_d ∈ Δ_D: push_f^D(φ(δ_s)(δ_d)) = φ(push_f^S(δ_s))(push_f^D(δ_d)). Similarly for quality interactions: push_f^Q(ψ(δ_s)(δ_q)) = ψ(push_f^S(δ_s))(push_f^Q(δ_q))
theorems.proof_strategy: Case analysis on each (operator, delta-sort, interaction-map) combination. For each triple, expand definitions under bag semantics and verify commutativity. Core lemma: interaction homomorphisms preserve SQL operator semantics
theorems.proof_sketch: For SELECT with ADD_COLUMN: push_SELECT^D(φ(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d))(INSERT(R))) = push_SELECT^D(INSERT(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)))) = INSERT(π_cols(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)))). If c ∉ select_cols: = INSERT(π_cols(R)) = φ(ε_S)(INSERT(π_cols(R))) = φ(push_SELECT^S(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d)))(push_SELECT^D(INSERT(R))). If c ∈ select_cols: = INSERT(π_cols∪{c}(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)))) = φ(ADD_COLUMN(output_name(c),τ,output_pos(c),d))(INSERT(π_cols(R))) = φ(push_SELECT^S(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d)))(push_SELECT^D(INSERT(R))). Similar analysis for remaining 47 combinations
key_lemmas: L1: Schema projection commutes with tuple extension
L2: Type coercion preserves relational operations
L3: Quality constraint scoping is preserved under schema evolution
theorems.novelty_rating: 7/10 - Hexagonal coherence for three-sorted algebra with cross-sort interactions is novel. Pattern recognition from category theory but instantiation for relational repair is new
theorems.engineering_consequence: Enables compositional reasoning about multi-stage repair. Without coherence, each pipeline would require custom analysis. With coherence, repair plans compose mechanically

theorems.name: Bounded Commutation with Decidable Fragment
theorems.statement: For pipeline G ∈ Fragment F, compound perturbation σ, repair plan R = generate_repair_plan(G, σ): apply(R, state(G)) = recompute(evolve(G, σ)). For G ∉ F: ||apply(R, state(G)) - recompute(evolve(G, σ))||₁ ≤ ε(G, σ) where ε is computable in polynomial time. Fragment membership is_in_fragment_F(G) is decidable via syntax analysis
theorems.proof_strategy: Structural induction on pipeline DAG. Base case: source nodes (trivial). Inductive step: coherence condition implies local commutativity at each stage. For bounded case: backward error analysis on non-deterministic operations
theorems.proof_sketch: Perfect case: By induction on topological order. For leaf node v with operator f, incoming repair δ, and coherence T1: apply(push_f(δ), f(state_in)) = f(apply(δ, state_in)) = f(recompute(evolve(state_in, δ))) = recompute(evolve(f(state_in), push_f(δ))). Inductive step: composition of coherent operations is coherent. Bounded case: for floating-point aggregation, error ε ≤ (n·machine_epsilon)·max_value where n is aggregation depth. For ORDER BY with ties, ε = 0 (same result set, different order). Fragment decidability: syntax tree traversal checks forbidden constructs in O(|AST|)
key_lemmas: L5: Non-deterministic operations have bounded deviation
L6: Fragment F syntactic restrictions are complete
theorems.novelty_rating: 6/10 - Commutation theorems exist in IVM literature. Decidable fragment characterization for SQL and computable error bounds are novel contributions
theorems.engineering_consequence: Provides the guarantee no existing tool offers: machine-checkable correctness certificates. System reports 'repair provably correct' or 'repair within bound ε=0.003' to users

theorems.name: DBSP Encoding Impossibility
theorems.statement: No encoding F: SchemaDeltas → Z-setDeltas preserves both type safety and incrementality. Formally: ∀F, either ∃δ_s: F(δ_s) requires dynamic type dispatch (loses type safety) or ∃δ_s: cost(apply(F(δ_s))) = O(|D|) not O(|δ_s|) (loses incrementality)
theorems.proof_strategy: Proof by exhaustive case analysis on four encoding strategies: (1) universal tuple type, (2) tagged unions, (3) deletion-reinsertion, (4) meta-table encoding. Show each violates at least one preservation property
theorems.proof_sketch: Universal tuple (Map<String,Any>): F(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ)) = INSERT({c: default(τ)}) but downstream operations require runtime type checking for τ, losing compile-time type safety. Tagged union (Either<Schema1,Schema2>): F(ADD_COLUMN) requires tagging all existing tuples with schema version, cost O(|D|) not O(1). Deletion-reinsertion: F(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ)) = DELETE(all_tuples) + INSERT(extend_all_tuples(c,τ)), cost O(|D|). Meta-table: F tracks schema separately but column operations require joining with data table, again O(|D|). In each case, either type parametricity is broken or incremental cost bounds are violated
key_lemmas: L7: DBSP parametricity requires fixed tuple types
L8: Schema operations necessarily affect all data
L9: Type safety and incrementality are conflicting requirements for schema deltas
theorems.novelty_rating: 5/10 - Core observation (fixed types vs schema evolution) is simple. Formal proof covering all encoding strategies and parametricity argument provides substance
theorems.engineering_consequence: Justifies three-sorted design architecturally. Answers reviewer objection 'why not extend DBSP?' - shows it's fundamental limitation not feature gap

theorems.name: Cost-Optimal Repair Planning
theorems.statement: (a) Optimal repair planning is NP-hard via reduction from weighted set cover. (b) For acyclic topologies, optimal plans computable in O(|V|·k²·|Δ|) via dynamic programming where |Δ| is delta representation size. (c) For general topologies, (ln k + 1)-approximation via LP relaxation
theorems.proof_strategy: NP-hardness by reduction: given weighted set cover instance (U, S, w), construct pipeline where stages correspond to sets, dependencies to containment, and repair requires covering all affected nodes. DP optimality via subproblem optimal substructure at annihilation boundaries
theorems.proof_sketch: NP-hardness: Given set cover (U={u₁,...,uₙ}, S={S₁,...,Sₘ}, w:S→ℝ), construct DAG with nodes V = U ∪ {root}, edges (root, uᵢ) and (uᵢ, Sⱼ) iff uᵢ ∈ Sⱼ. Perturbation at root, repair cost c(Sⱼ) = w(Sⱼ). Optimal repair plan corresponds to minimum weight set cover. DP correctness: for acyclic G, optimal repair plan for node v is min over {repair v directly + optimal plans for unaffected children} ∪ {optimal plans for all children}. Annihilation boundaries preserve optimal substructure since annihilated subtrees have zero cost
key_lemmas: L10: Set cover reduces to repair planning
L12: LP relaxation maintains coverage constraints
theorems.novelty_rating: 4/10 - Planning complexity results are standard. Delta annihilation integration and multi-sort cost model provide incremental novelty
theorems.engineering_consequence: Provides theoretical foundation for repair planner. DP algorithm guarantees optimality for 90%+ of real pipelines (acyclic). LP fallback handles remaining cases with approximation bounds

theorems.name: Delta Annihilation Soundness
theorems.statement: For all operators f, deltas δ: annihilates(f, δ) = true ⟺ push_f(δ) = 0. Annihilation detection is sound (no false positives) and complete for deterministic operators in fragment F
theorems.proof_strategy: Case analysis on annihilation rules per operator. Soundness: show annihilates(f,δ)=true implies push_f(δ)=0. Completeness: show push_f(δ)=0 implies annihilates(f,δ)=true for fragment F operators
theorems.proof_sketch: SELECT soundness: annihilates(SELECT, (ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d), δ_d, δ_q)) = true when c ∉ select_columns. Then push_SELECT^S(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d)) = ε_S and push_SELECT^D(φ(ADD_COLUMN(c,τ,p,d))(δ_d)) = push_SELECT^D(INSERT(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)))) = INSERT(π_cols(extend_tuples(R, c, p, eval_default(τ,d)))) = INSERT(π_cols(R)) = push_SELECT^D(δ_d) when c ∉ select_columns, so net delta is zero. Similar analysis for JOIN (empty inputs), FILTER (unsatisfiable predicates), GROUP_BY (zero-contribution deltas). Completeness follows by contrapositive: if push_f(δ) ≠ 0, then some component affects output, so annihilation rules detect non-zero cases
key_lemmas: L13: Column independence preserves zero deltas
L15: Unsatisfiable filters produce empty results
theorems.novelty_rating: 6/10 - Annihilation analysis for IVM exists but not for multi-sorted deltas with interaction homomorphisms. Soundness/completeness proof is novel
theorems.engineering_consequence: Guarantees that annihilation-based pruning never affects correctness. Enables aggressive optimization with formal safety bounds

theorems.name: Interaction Composition Associativity
theorems.statement: Compound perturbation composition is associative: (σ₁ ∘ σ₂) ∘ σ₃ = σ₁ ∘ (σ₂ ∘ σ₃) for all σᵢ = (δ_sᵢ, δ_dᵢ, δ_qᵢ). Sequential application produces identical results regardless of grouping
theorems.proof_strategy: Direct algebraic verification using homomorphism properties. Show that interaction terms φ(δ_s1)(φ(δ_s2)(δ_d3)) = φ(δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2)(δ_d3) from homomorphism composition
theorems.proof_sketch: Let σ₁ = (δ_s1, δ_d1, δ_q1), σ₂ = (δ_s2, δ_d2, δ_q2), σ₃ = (δ_s3, δ_d3, δ_q3). Left association: (σ₁ ∘ σ₂) ∘ σ₃ = (δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2, φ(δ_s1)(δ_d2) + δ_d1, ψ(δ_s1)(δ_q2) ⊔ δ_q1) ∘ σ₃ = (δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2 ∘ δ_s3, φ(δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2)(δ_d3) + φ(δ_s1)(δ_d2) + δ_d1, ψ(δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2)(δ_q3) ⊔ ψ(δ_s1)(δ_q2) ⊔ δ_q1). Right association: σ₁ ∘ (σ₂ ∘ σ₃) = σ₁ ∘ (δ_s2 ∘ δ_s3, φ(δ_s2)(δ_d3) + δ_d2, ψ(δ_s2)(δ_q3) ⊔ δ_q2) = (δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2 ∘ δ_s3, φ(δ_s1)(φ(δ_s2)(δ_d3) + δ_d2) + δ_d1, ψ(δ_s1)(ψ(δ_s2)(δ_q3) ⊔ δ_q2) ⊔ δ_q1). Equality follows from: φ(δ_s1 ∘ δ_s2) = φ(δ_s1) ∘ φ(δ_s2) (homomorphism), φ(δ_s1) distributes over +, ψ preserves lattice joins
key_lemmas: L18: Group operations distribute over homomorphisms
theorems.novelty_rating: 5/10 - Associativity proofs are algebraic routine. Multi-sorted interaction composition context provides domain novelty
theorems.engineering_consequence: Enables flexible compound perturbation handling. System can decompose complex perturbations arbitrarily without affecting semantics

algorithms.name: PROPAGATE - Delta Propagation Through Pipeline DAG
algorithms.pseudocode:
ALGORITHM PROPAGATE(G, σ, v_src):
  // Initialize delta map with identity deltas
  Δ := Map<Node, (Δ_S, Δ_D, Δ_Q)>()
  for each v ∈ V do
    Δ[v] := (ε_S, 0_D, ⊥_Q)
  
  // Set source perturbation
  Δ[v_src] := σ
  
  // Topological traversal
  topo_order := TOPOLOGICAL_SORT(G)
  
  for each v ∈ topo_order do
    if v == v_src then continue
    
    // Aggregate incoming deltas from all predecessors
    δ_s_agg, δ_d_agg, δ_q_agg := ε_S, 0_D, ⊥_Q
    
    for each u ∈ PREDECESSORS(v) do
      (δ_s_u, δ_d_u, δ_q_u) := Δ[u]
      
      // Apply interaction homomorphisms before aggregation
      δ_d_transformed := φ(δ_s_u)(δ_d_u)
      δ_q_transformed := ψ(δ_s_u)(δ_q_u)
      
      // Aggregate using sort-specific operations
      δ_s_agg := δ_s_agg ∘ δ_s_u        // Monoid composition
      δ_d_agg := δ_d_agg + δ_d_transformed  // Group addition
      δ_q_agg := δ_q_agg ⊔ δ_q_transformed  // Lattice join
    
    // Push through operator at node v
    f_v := OPERATOR(v)
    δ_s_out := PUSH_SCHEMA(f_v, δ_s_agg)
    δ_d_out := PUSH_DATA(f_v, δ_d_agg)
    δ_q_out := PUSH_QUALITY(f_v, δ_q_agg)
    
    Δ[v] := (δ_s_out, δ_d_out, δ_q_out)
  
  return Δ
algorithms.input: Pipeline DAG G = (V, E, λ, τ), compound perturbation σ = (δ_s, δ_d, δ_q), source node v_src ∈ V
algorithms.output: Map Δ: V → (Δ_S × Δ_D × Δ_Q) where Δ[v] is the propagated delta at node v
algorithms.correctness: Hexagonal coherence (T1) ensures interaction homomorphisms preserve operator semantics. Topological order guarantees all dependencies are processed before dependent nodes
algorithms.time_complexity: O(|V| + |E| + |V|·|C|·|Δ|) where |C| is average schema size and |Δ| is delta representation size
algorithms.space_complexity: O(|V|·|C|·|Δ|) for delta storage at each node

algorithms.name: ANNIHILATE - Delta Annihilation Detection
algorithms.input: SQL operator f ∈ Op, compound delta δ = (δ_s, δ_d, δ_q)
algorithms.correctness: Soundness proven by T5: annihilates(f,δ)=true ⟹ push_f(δ)=0. Conservative for unrecognized patterns
algorithms.time_complexity: O(|δ_s|·|C| + |δ_d|) for column dependency analysis and data delta inspection

algorithms.name: DP-REPAIR - Dynamic Programming Repair Planner
algorithms.input: Pipeline DAG G = (V, E), propagated deltas Δ: V → (Δ_S × Δ_D × Δ_Q)
algorithms.output: Repair plan with minimum cost and required recomputation actions
algorithms.correctness: Optimal substructure holds at annihilation boundaries (T4). DP correctly explores all repair options
algorithms.time_complexity: O(|V|·k²·|Δ|) where k is maximum fan-out and |Δ| is delta size

algorithms.name: LP-REPAIR - Linear Programming Repair Planner
algorithms.input: Pipeline DAG G = (V, E), propagated deltas Δ: V → (Δ_S × Δ_D × Δ_Q)
algorithms.output: Approximate repair plan with (ln k + 1)-approximation guarantee
algorithms.correctness: LP relaxation preserves coverage constraints. Randomized rounding maintains expected cost. Greedy patch ensures feasibility
algorithms.time_complexity: O(|V|³) for LP solving + O(|V|·k) for rounding and patching

algorithms.name: COMPOSE - Compound Perturbation Composition
algorithms.pseudocode:
ALGORITHM COMPOSE(σ₁, σ₂):
  // Extract components
  (δ_s1, δ_d1, δ_q1) := σ₁
  (δ_s2, δ_d2, δ_q2) := σ₂
  
  // Schema composition (monoid operation)
  δ_s_composed := COMPOSE_SCHEMA_DELTAS(δ_s1, δ_s2)
  
  // Data composition with interaction homomorphism
  δ_d_transformed := APPLY_INTERACTION_PHI(δ_s1, δ_d2)
  δ_d_composed := GROUP_ADD(δ_d1, δ_d_transformed)
  
  // Quality composition with interaction homomorphism  
  δ_q_transformed := APPLY_INTERACTION_PSI(δ_s1, δ_q2)
  δ_q_composed := LATTICE_JOIN(δ_q1, δ_q_transformed)
  
  return (δ_s_composed, δ_d_composed, δ_q_composed)

SUBROUTINE COMPOSE_SCHEMA_DELTAS(δ_s1, δ_s2):
  // Apply δ_s1 then δ_s2 with conflict resolution
  result := []
  
  for each op1 ∈ δ_s1 do
    result.append(op1)
  
  for each op2 ∈ δ_s2 do
    if CONFLICTS_WITH_PRIOR(op2, result):
      result := RESOLVE_CONFLICT(result, op2)
    else:
      result.append(op2)
  
  return result

SUBROUTINE APPLY_INTERACTION_PHI(δ_s, δ_d):
  // Transform data delta through schema homomorphism
  for each schema_op ∈ δ_s do
    δ_d := APPLY_SCHEMA_TO_DATA(schema_op, δ_d)
  return δ_d

SUBROUTINE APPLY_INTERACTION_PSI(δ_s, δ_q):
  // Transform quality delta through schema homomorphism
  for each schema_op ∈ δ_s do
    δ_q := APPLY_SCHEMA_TO_QUALITY(schema_op, δ_q)
  return δ_q
algorithms.input: Two compound perturbations σ₁ = (δ_s1, δ_d1, δ_q1), σ₂ = (δ_s2, δ_d2, δ_q2)
algorithms.output: Composed perturbation σ₁ ∘ σ₂ with interaction effects
algorithms.correctness: Associativity proven by T6. Interaction homomorphisms preserve cross-sort dependencies
algorithms.time_complexity: O(|δ_s1|·|δ_s2| + |δ_d|·|C| + |δ_q|·|Q|) for conflict resolution and interaction application

algorithms.name: VERIFY-FRAGMENT - Fragment F Membership Testing
algorithms.input: Pipeline DAG G = (V, E, λ, τ) with SQL queries at each node
algorithms.output: (Boolean, String) indicating fragment membership and violation reason if any
algorithms.correctness: Syntactic analysis is sound: all detected violations break determinism or exact arithmetic. Conservative: may reject valid deterministic queries with complex patterns
algorithms.time_complexity: O(Σ_{v∈V} |AST(v)|) - linear in total AST size across all stages

algorithms.name: EXECUTE-REPAIR - Repair Plan Execution with Checkpointing
algorithms.pseudocode:
ALGORITHM EXECUTE_REPAIR(G, repair_plan, checkpoint_manager):
  actions := repair_plan.actions
  execution_order := TOPOLOGICAL_SORT(EXTRACT_NODES(actions))
  
  // Create execution checkpoint
  checkpoint_id := checkpoint_manager.CREATE_CHECKPOINT()
  
  try:
    for each node ∈ execution_order do
      if RECOMPUTE(node) ∈ actions:
        
        // Pre-execution validation
        if not VALIDATE_NODE_PRECONDITIONS(node):
          throw REPAIR_EXECUTION_ERROR(f"Preconditions failed for {node}")
        
        // Execute recomputation
        LOG(INFO, f"Recomputing node {node}")
        start_time := NOW()
        
        query := GET_QUERY(node)
        input_tables := GET_INPUT_TABLES(node)
        output_table := GET_OUTPUT_TABLE(node)
        
        # Execute SQL with timeout protection
        result := EXECUTE_SQL_WITH_TIMEOUT(query, input_tables, TIMEOUT_SECONDS)
        
        # Atomic table replacement
        temp_table := CREATE_TEMP_TABLE(output_table)
        INSERT_INTO(temp_table, result)
        VALIDATE_OUTPUT_SCHEMA(temp_table, EXPECTED_SCHEMA(node))
        ATOMIC_SWAP(output_table, temp_table)
        
        execution_time := NOW() - start_time
        LOG(INFO, f"Node {node} recomputed in {execution_time}ms")
        
        # Update repair statistics
        repair_plan.record_execution(node, execution_time, len(result))
    
    # Validation phase: verify repair correctness
    if FRAGMENT_F_PIPELINE(G):
      # Perfect correctness expected
      validation_result := VALIDATE_PERFECT_REPAIR(G, repair_plan)
      if not validation_result.correct:
        throw REPAIR_CORRECTNESS_ERROR(validation_result.error_msg)
    else:
      # Bounded correctness: check error bounds
      deviation := COMPUTE_REPAIR_DEVIATION(G, repair_plan)
      error_bound := COMPUTE_ERROR_BOUND(G, repair_plan.perturbation)
      if deviation > error_bound:
        throw REPAIR_CORRECTNESS_ERROR(f"Deviation {deviation} exceeds bound {error_bound}")
    
    # Commit checkpoint
    checkpoint_manager.COMMIT_CHECKPOINT(checkpoint_id)
    
    return REPAIR_SUCCESS(
      nodes_recomputed=len(execution_order),
      total_time=SUM(repair_plan.execution_times),
      correctness_guarantee=FRAGMENT_F_PIPELINE(G) ? "PERFECT" : f"BOUNDED(ε={error_bound})"
    )
  
  except Exception as e:
    # Rollback on any failure
    LOG(ERROR, f"Repair execution failed: {e}")
    checkpoint_manager.ROLLBACK_CHECKPOINT(checkpoint_id)
    return REPAIR_FAILURE(error=str(e), checkpoint_restored=true)

SUBROUTINE VALIDATE_PERFECT_REPAIR(G, repair_plan):
  # For Fragment F pipelines: repair result must equal full recomputation
  perturbation := repair_plan.perturbation
  
  # Execute full recomputation as ground truth
  G_evolved := EVOLVE_PIPELINE(G, perturbation)
  truth_results := EXECUTE_FULL_PIPELINE(G_evolved)
  
  # Compare with repair results
  repair_results := EXTRACT_CURRENT_RESULTS(G)
  
  for each output_table ∈ GET_OUTPUT_TABLES(G) do
    truth_data := truth_results[output_table]
    repair_data := repair_results[output_table]
    
    if not MULTISET_EQUAL(truth_data, repair_data):
      return VALIDATION_FAILURE(f"Mismatch in {output_table}: expected {len(truth_data)} rows, got {len(repair_data)}")
  
  return VALIDATION_SUCCESS()
algorithms.input: Pipeline DAG G, repair plan with recomputation actions, checkpoint manager for rollback
algorithms.output: Repair execution result with correctness guarantees and performance statistics
algorithms.correctness: Atomic execution with checkpointing ensures all-or-nothing semantics. Validation against theoretical guarantees (perfect for Fragment F, bounded otherwise)
algorithms.time_complexity: O(Σ_{v∈repair_actions} COST(v)) - sum of recomputation costs plus validation overhead
algorithms.space_complexity: O(max_table_size) for temporary tables and checkpointing

predictions.claim: Perfect Commutation in Fragment F: For SQL pipelines in fragment F, apply(repair_plan, state) = recompute(evolve(pipeline, perturbation)) with 100% correctness
predictions.measurement: Execute 10,000 random perturbations on TPC-DS pipelines verified to be in Fragment F. Compare repair results with full recomputation via row-by-row DuckDB result set comparison
predictions.falsification: Any single counterexample where repair output ≠ full recomputation output for a pipeline verified to be in fragment F
predictions.target: 100% correctness for all Fragment F pipelines

predictions.claim: 2-5x Cost Reduction via Delta Annihilation: Algebraic repair achieves 50-80% stage pruning compared to dbt selective recomputation on realistic schema evolution traces
predictions.measurement: Count stages executed by repair plans vs dbt --select on 500 synthetic topologies with column additions (40%), type widenings (25%), renames (20%), drops (15%)
predictions.falsification: Cost ratio ≥ 0.8 (less than 20% savings) averaged across perturbation types, or dbt outperforms algebraic approach on any major perturbation class
predictions.target: 2-5× cost reduction (cost ratio 0.2-0.5) vs modern dbt with column-level lineage

predictions.claim: Fragment F Coverage 60-75%: Fragment F covers 60-75% of stages in realistic ETL pipelines (reduced from original >80% claim after red team critique)
predictions.measurement: Syntactic analysis of TPC-DS corpus, dbt-core examples, and 50+ real-world dbt projects from GitHub. Count stages satisfying Fragment F restrictions
predictions.falsification: Coverage ≤ 50% on any representative benchmark suite, or syntactic restrictions eliminate core ETL patterns
predictions.target: 60-75% coverage with clear characterization of excluded patterns (ORDER BY, statistical aggregates, UDFs)

predictions.claim: Interactive Planning Speed: Repair plan generation completes in <1 second P95 latency for pipelines with ≤500 stages
predictions.measurement: P95 latency across 1000 planning requests on synthetic DAGs with varying fan-out (k=2,5,10) and sizes (50,200,500 stages)
predictions.falsification: P95 latency >2 seconds for any topology configuration within size limits
predictions.target: <1s P95 for 500-stage pipelines, <100ms P95 for 50-stage pipelines

predictions.claim: Compound Perturbation Necessity: In 10-15% of real schema evolution episodes, simultaneous schema/data/quality perturbations produce different outcomes than sequential handling
predictions.measurement: Analysis of 100+ GitHub schema migration traces (Rails/Alembic) with synthetic data/quality perturbations injected at migration boundaries. Compare compound vs sequential repair results
predictions.falsification: <8% of cases show order-dependent results, or inability to collect sufficient real-world migration traces
predictions.target: 10-15% of episodes demonstrate compound perturbation necessity with measurable correctness differences

predictions.claim: SQL Lineage Recall ≥95%: Column-level lineage analysis achieves ≥95% recall on TPC-DS queries without falling back to all-to-all dependency
predictions.measurement: Manual ground truth construction for 99 TPC-DS queries by 3 independent annotators (κ>0.8). Automated precision/recall calculation vs ground truth
predictions.falsification: Recall <90% or precision <85% on any major SQL operator class (SELECT/JOIN/GROUP BY/FILTER/WINDOW)
predictions.target: ≥95% recall, ≥90% precision on single-dialect SQL analysis

predictions.claim: Interaction Homomorphism Coherence: All 8 SQL operators satisfy hexagonal coherence with 0 violations in property-based testing
predictions.measurement: 1M+ randomized delta combinations per operator via Hypothesis framework. Test coherence condition for each (operator, schema-delta, data-delta) triple
predictions.falsification: Any reproducible coherence violation that cannot be attributed to implementation bugs, or systematic test case generation bias
predictions.target: 0 coherence violations across 8M+ total test cases (8 operators × 1M cases each)

predictions.claim: DBSP Encoding Impossibility: No encoding of schema deltas into DBSP Z-sets preserves both type safety and incrementality for our four canonical encoding strategies
predictions.measurement: Formal proof construction + concrete counterexamples for tagged union, deletion-reinsertion, universal tuple, and meta-table approaches
predictions.falsification: Discovery of type-preserving, incremental encoding that maintains O(|Δ|) cost bounds, or proof reduction to trivial type system observation
predictions.target: Formal impossibility proof covering all four strategies with concrete complexity/type-safety violations demonstrated

predictions.claim: Bounded Error Outside Fragment F: For pipelines outside fragment F, repair deviation is bounded and computable: |repair - truth| ≤ ε(pipeline, perturbation)
predictions.measurement: Inject controlled non-determinism (ORDER BY with ties, floating-point GROUP BY aggregates) and measure output deviation vs theoretical bounds
predictions.falsification: Unbounded error growth in any tested scenario, or uncomputability of ε for common non-deterministic patterns
predictions.target: Computable error bounds ε for all tested non-Fragment F patterns with empirical validation within 2× of theoretical bounds

predictions.claim: Annihilation Rate Correlation: Stage pruning via annihilation correlates strongly (r>0.7) with pipeline fan-out structure and perturbation locality
predictions.measurement: Regression analysis across 500 topologies with controlled perturbation injection points. Predict annihilation rate from graph features
predictions.falsification: Correlation coefficient <0.5 or inability to predict annihilation effectiveness from topology metrics
predictions.target: Strong correlation (r>0.7) enabling annihilation rate prediction from static pipeline analysis

predictions.claim: Property-Based Test Coverage: 10M+ property-based tests achieve comprehensive delta combination coverage across all operator/sort combinations
predictions.measurement: Hypothesis test execution with coverage tracking and shrinking on failures. Monitor delta diversity and edge case coverage
predictions.falsification: Systematic gaps in test coverage, repeated identical test cases, or shrinking failures revealing algebraic inconsistencies
predictions.target: 10M+ distinct test cases with >95% coverage of realistic delta combinations

predictions.claim: Three-Sorted vs Two-Sorted Advantage: Three-sorted algebra handles 15-25% more repair scenarios correctly than two-sorted approach
predictions.measurement: Head-to-head comparison on compound perturbations involving quality constraint violations. Compare correctness and error rates
predictions.falsification: <10% scenario coverage advantage, or equivalent correctness achievable with quality-as-tagged-data encoding
predictions.target: 15-25% correctness advantage demonstrating quality sort necessity

predictions.claim: Planning Algorithm Optimality: DP planner produces provably optimal repair plans for acyclic topologies under delta-algebraic cost model
predictions.measurement: Exhaustive comparison with brute force enumeration on small DAGs (≤20 nodes). Verify optimality gap = 0 for all acyclic test cases
predictions.falsification: Any suboptimal plan on acyclic topology, or DP algorithm producing plans with cost >1% above brute force optimum
predictions.target: Provably optimal plans for 100% of acyclic topologies with mathematical verification

predictions.claim: Real-World Schema Trace Validity: Synthetic perturbation distributions match real-world schema evolution patterns from 100+ open-source projects
predictions.measurement: Statistical comparison (KS test, p>0.05) of perturbation type frequencies and size distributions between synthetic and real traces
predictions.falsification: Significant distribution differences (p<0.01) that cannot be explained by domain-specific biases
predictions.target: Statistically indistinguishable distributions (p>0.05) for major perturbation types across domains

predictions.claim: Quality Delta Inference Accuracy: Batch quality monitoring detects 90%+ of injected quality violations with <5% false positive rate
predictions.measurement: Controlled data quality degradation experiments with KS test + PSI monitoring across different statistical profiles
predictions.falsification: Detection rate <85% or false positive rate >10% on any common data distribution pattern
predictions.target: ≥90% detection rate, ≤5% false positive rate across diverse quality violation types

predictions.claim: Memory Usage Scalability: Peak memory usage grows sub-linearly O(|V|·log|V|·|Δ|) with pipeline size for delta algebra representation
predictions.measurement: Memory profiling across synthetic topologies from 10-1000 stages with constant perturbation complexity. Fit growth curve to empirical data
predictions.falsification: Super-linear memory growth (>O(|V|^1.2)) or memory exhaustion before 500 stages on standard hardware (32GB RAM)
predictions.target: Sub-linear memory scaling enabling 1000+ stage pipeline support within memory bounds

predictions.claim: Compound Perturbation Composition Correctness: Sequential application produces identical results regardless of decomposition strategy (associativity)
predictions.measurement: Randomized composition trees with algebraic equivalence checking. Test all possible groupings for 3-5 perturbation sequences
predictions.falsification: Any composition order producing different final states for deterministic pipelines in Fragment F
predictions.target: Perfect associativity for 100% of deterministic composition scenarios

predictions.claim: Baseline Performance Gap: Algebraic approach outperforms rule-based heuristic repair by 3-10× in cost efficiency while maintaining higher correctness
predictions.measurement: Head-to-head evaluation against sqlmesh-style hand-written repair rules and modern dbt with column-level lineage on diverse perturbation suites
predictions.falsification: <2× cost advantage or lower correctness rate than best heuristic baseline
predictions.target: 3-10× cost improvement with ≥95% correctness vs ≤90% for heuristic approaches

baselines.name: Full Recomputation
baselines.description: Naive baseline that rebuilds entire pipeline from scratch, ignoring perturbation locality. Represents worst-case repair strategy with perfect correctness but maximum cost
baselines.implementation: Execute all stages in topological order using DuckDB. No dependency analysis or selective execution. Serves as correctness oracle for Fragment F validation
baselines.expected_outcome: Perfect correctness (100%), maximum cost (baseline ratio = 1.0). Demonstrates upper bound on repair cost and provides ground truth for correctness verification

baselines.name: Modern dbt with Column-Level Lineage
baselines.description: State-of-practice baseline using dbt 1.6+ with column-level lineage analysis and --select flag for selective recomputation. Represents current production capabilities
baselines.implementation: Deploy dbt-core with column lineage enabled. Use dbt --select +modified_model+ for downstream propagation. Include compilation time and dependency resolution overhead in cost measurement
baselines.expected_outcome: Good correctness for simple perturbations (90-95%), 2-5× higher cost than algebraic approach due to inability to detect annihilation opportunities

baselines.name: DBSP Data-Only IVM
baselines.description: State-of-art incremental view maintenance using DBSP circuits with fixed schema. Represents best-case performance for data-only changes while demonstrating schema evolution limitations
baselines.implementation: Construct DBSP circuits for TPC-DS queries using dbsp-rs. Apply data deltas as Z-set changes. Schema changes cause circuit reconstruction (fallback to full recomputation)
baselines.expected_outcome: Excellent performance for data-only changes (10-100× speedup), complete failure on schema evolution. Demonstrates expressiveness gap our approach addresses

baselines.name: Naive Incremental (Direct Effects Only)
baselines.description: Simplest incremental strategy that recomputes stages with modified inputs but ignores transitive effects. Tests necessity of propagation analysis
baselines.implementation: Identify directly affected stages and recompute them. Do not propagate changes downstream unless input tables are explicitly modified. No delta analysis
baselines.expected_outcome: Fast execution (50% cost) but incorrect results (60-70% correctness). Demonstrates why propagation analysis is essential

baselines.name: Rule-Based Heuristic Repair
baselines.description: Hand-engineered repair logic using pattern matching on perturbation types. Represents pre-algebraic engineering approach with domain-specific optimizations
baselines.implementation: Pattern matching on perturbation types: column add → default value injection + downstream recomputation, type change → casting pipeline + affected stage recomputation, data correction → selective propagation based on hardcoded rules
baselines.expected_outcome: Reasonable performance for common cases (70-80% cost efficiency), failure on compound perturbations (75-85% correctness). Shows algebra necessity for compositionality

baselines.name: No-Algebra Ablation (Critical Control)
baselines.description: Our system architecture without delta algebra - same SQL analyzer, dependency graph, and infrastructure but heuristic planning instead of algebraic reasoning
baselines.implementation: Use identical SQL parsing, lineage analysis, and execution engine. Replace algebraic planner with heuristic rules: recompute downstream for schema changes, use dbt-style selective recomputation for data changes, ignore quality deltas
baselines.expected_outcome: 80-90% of algebraic capability without theory. This is the key experiment: if gap is <20%, algebra's value is primarily theoretical elegance. If gap is >30%, algebra provides measurable engineering benefit

complexity_analysis.propagation: O(|V| + |E| + |V|·|C|·|Δ|) where |V| is pipeline size, |E| is dependencies, |C| is average schema size, |Δ| is delta representation size. Accounts for interaction homomorphism application at each stage.
complexity_analysis.planning_dag: O(|V|·k²·|Δ|) for acyclic topologies using dynamic programming, where k ≤ 20 is maximum fan-out. Corrected from original O(|V|·k²) to include delta size factor as identified by red team.
complexity_analysis.planning_general: O(|V|³) for LP relaxation + O(|V|·k) for randomized rounding. Provides (ln k + 1)-approximation for general topologies with cycles.
complexity_analysis.annihilation: O(|δ_s|·|C| + |δ_d|) per stage for column dependency analysis and data delta inspection. Critical optimization enabling 2-5× cost reduction through stage pruning.
complexity_analysis.fragment_check: O(Σ_{v∈V} |AST(v)|) for syntactic analysis of all stage queries. Linear in total AST size, enables runtime verification of commutation preconditions.
complexity_analysis.total_pipeline: O(|V|·k²·|Δ| + |V|³) for planning + O(Σ_{v∈repair_actions} COST(v)) for execution. Planning overhead typically <1% of execution cost for realistic delta sizes.

modules.name: SQL Semantic Analyzer
modules.description: Parse SQL queries and extract column-level lineage using sqlglot. Handles PostgreSQL dialect with 8 core operators. Conservative sound over-approximation for unrecognized patterns

modules.name: Delta Algebra Engine
modules.description: Core three-sorted algebra implementation with interaction homomorphisms φ and ψ. Handles schema/data/quality delta composition and propagation

modules.name: Repair Planner
modules.description: DP algorithm for acyclic topologies, LP relaxation for general case. Delta annihilation optimization for cost reduction

modules.name: Execution Engine
modules.description: DuckDB-based repair execution with checkpointing and rollback. Atomic table replacement and correctness validation

modules.name: Quality Monitor
modules.description: Batch quality monitoring using KS test and PSI for distribution shift detection. Generates quality deltas for constraint violations

implementation_strategy.testing_strategy: Property-based testing for algebra (Hypothesis framework, 10M+ test cases). Integration testing with TPC-DS corpus. Correctness validation against full recomputation baselines. Performance benchmarking on synthetic topologies.

risk_assessment.mitigation: Focus on 5 critical operators with formal proofs. Property-based testing for remaining operators. Conservative fallbacks for edge cases

risk_assessment.risk: Fragment F Coverage Lower Than Expected
risk_assessment.mitigation: Already reduced target to 60-75%. Honest reporting of exclusions. Bounded commutation still valuable outside Fragment F
Demote to design motivation if proof lacks depth. Three-sorted algebra stands on own merits for compound perturbations
Conservative cost models. Focus on annihilation benefits. Fair comparison with modern dbt capabilities
Two-sorted fallback strategy. Incremental delivery. Core algebra before full system integration
Sound over-approximation strategy. Target ≥95% recall with explicit fallback handling

related_work.ivm: Incremental View Maintenance (IVM) literature provides delta propagation foundations but focuses on single-sorted data deltas. DBSP offers sophisticated Z-set algebra but cannot handle schema evolution due to type parametricity. Our three-sorted approach extends IVM to handle schema changes through interaction homomorphisms - a capability no existing IVM system provides.
related_work.schema_evolution: Schema evolution research (Curino et al., Claypool et al.) focuses on migration correctness but treats data as passive payload. They provide schema mapping operators but no integration with incremental computation. We extend this by making schema deltas first-class algebraic objects that interact with data/quality through homomorphisms.
related_work.data_quality: Data quality monitoring (Great Expectations, Deequ) treats quality as separate concern from pipeline maintenance. Quality constraints are typically checked post-hoc without integration into repair planning. Our quality delta lattice enables compound reasoning about schema changes, data corrections, and quality constraint evolution in unified algebraic framework.
related_work.what_we_extend: We extend IVM with multi-sorted algebra, schema evolution with incremental computation semantics, and data quality with repair-aware constraint handling. The interaction homomorphisms φ and ψ are the novel algebraic mechanisms enabling this unification. No prior work provides formal composition rules for simultaneous schema/data/quality changes.

honest_assessment.novelty: 6-7/10. The three-sorted delta algebra with interaction homomorphisms is a novel application of known algebraic patterns to a new domain. Core mathematical techniques (homomorphisms, coherence conditions) are standard in category theory and universal algebra, but their instantiation for relational repair with complete formal definitions and engineering validation is new. The DBSP impossibility result adds theoretical depth if non-trivial, otherwise contributes little novelty.
honest_assessment.difficulty: 8-9/10. Multiple challenging components: (1) Complete formal definitions for 48 coherence conditions across operator/sort combinations, (2) Sound SQL analysis with ≥95% precision on real queries, (3) Efficient algorithms for NP-hard repair planning, (4) Property-based testing framework for algebraic verification, (5) Integration of theory with working system. Each component is individually difficult; their combination is very challenging.
honest_assessment.feasibility: 7/10 with mitigation strategies. Core algebra and basic repair are feasible. Risk factors: SQL analysis complexity (mitigated by conservative over-approximation), coherence verification scale (mitigated by focusing on 5 critical operators), real-world evaluation challenges (mitigated by synthetic benchmarks). Two-sorted fallback provides safety net if three-sorted proves too complex.
honest_assessment.best_paper_probability: 25-35% at VLDB. Strengths: addresses important practical problem, novel algebraic formulation, working system with evaluation. Risks: theoretical contribution may be seen as incremental application rather than fundamental advance, evaluation limited to single SQL dialect, baseline comparisons need careful fairness. Success depends on demonstrating clear engineering value of algebraic approach through ablation studies.
